question_text: According to Wikipedia, will Katinka Hosszú still hold the world record for 200m individual medley in long course (50 metres) swimming pools on 2025-12-28?

nodes.description: Katinka Hosszú currently holds the world record in 200m individual medley long course
Length of time world records typically endure in elite swimming
Rate at which swimming performances improve due to technique, training, and technology
Number of major international competitions where record attempts occur
Consistency of FINA rules regarding pool standards, swimwear, and timing
Whether Katinka Hosszú remains an active competitor or has retired
Katinka Hosszú still holds the world record for 200m individual medley in long course pools on 2025-12-28

edges.mechanism: Being the current record holder is a prerequisite to still holding the record in the future
Longer typical record durations increase the likelihood the current record survives until 2025-12-28
Faster performance improvement reduces the expected lifespan of records
More high-level competitions increase opportunities for record-breaking performances
Stable rules limit artificial performance spikes, slowing progression and extending record longevity
If Hosszú is retired, she cannot re-break her own record, increasing chances it will be overtaken

reasoning: Katinka Hosszú set the 200m IM world record in 2015, and it has stood for nearly a decade. While longevity of records in swimming has historically been a few years, technological and training advances continue to push performance. Given the frequency of elite competitions (Olympics, World Championships) and steady progression in women's medley events, the probability of the record standing until late 2025 is modest. Hosszú's retirement from top-level competition removes the possibility of her defending the record, further decreasing survival odds. Rule stability helps preserve records, but the pressure from rising competitors, especially from the US and China, makes a breakthrough likely. Thus, the probability is below 50% but not negligible.

probes.probe_text: What if the rate of swimming performance improvement has effectively plateaued due to physiological limits, making further gains from technique, training, and technology negligible? Despite past trends, elite times in the 200m individual medley have seen minimal improvement over the last decade, suggesting that human performance may be approaching its upper bound, rendering ongoing progression unlikely and thus diminishing its impact on record longevity.
probes.description: Rate at which swimming performances improve due to technique, training, and technology

probes.probe_text: What if world records in elite swimming are not primarily determined by historical longevity trends, but instead are broken in sudden bursts due to unforeseen technological advances, such as new swimwear materials or pool design, making past record durations a poor predictor of future survival? In that case, even a historically long-standing record might be highly vulnerable regardless of average longevity.
probes.description: Length of time world records typically endure in elite swimming

probes.probe_text: Recent changes in the international swimming calendar have reduced the number of major competitions from six to three per year due to consolidation of events under the World Aquatics Championships and Olympic cycle. With fewer high-level meets where record attempts are attempted under peak conditions, the rate of swimming progression has slowed, suggesting that elite competition frequency may not be driving performance improvement as strongly as assumed.
probes.description: Number of major international competitions where record attempts occur

probes.probe_text: Katinka Hosszú has officially retired from competitive swimming, but recent training logs and interviews suggest she may return to elite competition for a final season in 2025. If she comes out of retirement, her active status could allow her to defend or even re-break her own record, undermining the assumption that retirement permanently reduces her chances of retaining the record.
probes.description: Whether Katinka Hosszú remains an active competitor or has retired

probes.probe_text: A 2024 study published in the Journal of Sports Sciences analyzed the last 20 years of elite swimming performances and found that advancements in underwater dolphin kick techniques, combined with optimized training regimens using AI-driven analytics, have accelerated the rate of improvement in the 200m individual medley by 1.8% per year since 2020—double the rate observed in the previous decade. This suggests that swimming progression is occurring faster than historically assumed, increasing the likelihood of record-breaking performances before 2025-12-28.
probes.description: Rate at which swimming performances improve due to technique, training, and technology

probes.probe_text: A 2024 longitudinal study published in the Journal of Sports Performance Analysis found that world records in long-course swimming have lasted an average of 7.3 years since 2000, with records set after 2010 showing increased durability due to maturation of technique and stricter oversight of performance-enhancing technologies. The study concludes that records established in the last decade are likely to persist longer than previously expected, reinforcing the structural stability of existing benchmarks like Hosszú's.
probes.description: Length of time world records typically endure in elite swimming

probes.probe_text: Recent data from the past decade shows that despite steady improvements in training and technology, the rate of world record breaks in the 200m individual medley has slowed, with no new records set since 2015. This suggests that swimming progression may have diminishing returns at the elite level, where physiological limits constrain further gains, weakening the causal link between performance improvement and record longevity.
probes.description: Faster performance improvement reduces the expected lifespan of records

probes.probe_text: Recent data from World Aquatics shows that 80% of long course swimming records set between 2015 and 2020 were broken within five years, despite historically long record longevity in the 2000s. This accelerating trend suggests that past record longevity is no longer predictive of future survival, especially for records set before the latest wave of training and technology advances. As a result, relying on historical record longevity may overestimate the durability of Hosszú's 2015 record.
probes.description: Longer typical record durations increase the likelihood the current record survives until 2025-12-28

probes.probe_text: Even if Katinka Hosszú retires, she could still indirectly influence the likelihood of her record being broken by inspiring targeted training programs or pacing strategies in younger swimmers aiming to surpass her time, meaning retirement does not necessarily increase the vulnerability of the record. In some cases, retired record-holders' times become psychological benchmarks that actually slow down subsequent record progression due to their symbolic status.
probes.description: If Hosszú is retired, she cannot re-break her own record, increasing chances it will be overtaken

probes.probe_text: Suppose the world record in the 200m individual medley has remained unbroken for an unusually long time. This stagnation may trigger intensified focus from coaches, athletes, and sports scientists on cracking the record, leading to targeted innovations in training and technique. Could this mean that long-standing records actually accelerate swimming progression, rather than being merely eroded by it?
probes.description: Faster performance improvement reduces the expected lifespan of records

probes.probe_text: Imagine that, historically, world records in elite swimming were broken much more frequently—say, every 1.5 years on average instead of every 3—due to rapid advancements in training and swimwear. Would it still be as likely that Katinka Hosszú set and retained the 200m IM record in the first place, given that such a volatile environment would make any single record less likely to emerge and stabilize? This suggests that the typical longevity of records causally influences which athletes end up being record holders, as only in relatively stable periods do records persist long enough to be set and recognized.
probes.description: Potential link: Length of time world records typically endure in elite swimming -> Katinka Hosszú currently holds the world record in 200m individual medley long course

probes.probe_text: Suppose new data shows that world records in elite swimming are being broken 40% faster than in the past decade due to advances in training and underwater technology. How does this accelerated progression affect the likelihood that Katinka Hosszú remains an active competitor? Consider that shorter record longevity may demotivate elite swimmers from continuing if they perceive their achievements as more easily surpassed.
probes.description: Potential link: Length of time world records typically endure in elite swimming -> Whether Katinka Hosszú remains an active competitor or has retired

probes.probe_text: Consider the possibility of a technological advancement in swimwear material that significantly enhances buoyancy and reduces drag, approved by FINA before 2025. Such a change could accelerate performance improvements across elite swimming, increasing the likelihood that existing records, including Hosszú's, are broken even if other factors like rule stability and competition frequency remain unchanged. This represents a shock to the system not captured by the current network.
probes.description: A plausible factor not in the current network

probes.probe_text: Suppose a new technological advancement, such as a significantly more efficient swimsuit design, is approved by FINA in 2024. This leads to a wave of record-breaking performances across multiple swimming disciplines, including the 200m individual medley. How does this affect the likelihood that Katinka Hosszú's record will still stand by the end of 2025?

In 2024, a new training facility dedicated to adolescent swimmers was opened in Budapest, named in honor of Katinka Hosszú. The center focuses on developing young talent in individual medley events and has received significant government funding.
probes.description: Topically related but causally irrelevant information

probes.probe_text: In 2024, a new underwater camera system was introduced at major swimming events to enhance broadcast quality and provide real-time stroke analysis for commentators. While widely adopted for television coverage, the technology does not influence swimmer performance or timing accuracy.
probes.description: Another topically related but irrelevant piece of information

probe_results.probe_text: What if the rate of swimming performance improvement has effectively plateaued due to physiological limits, making further gains from technique, training, and technology negligible? Despite past trends, elite times in the 200m individual medley have seen minimal improvement over the last decade, suggesting that human performance may be approaching its upper bound, rendering ongoing progression unlikely and thus diminishing its impact on record longevity.
probe_results.reasoning: The challenge to 'swimming_progression' significantly affects a key causal path: if performance improvement has plateaued due to physiological limits, then the rate at which records are broken—especially in technically demanding events like the 200m individual medley—slows substantially. This weakens the link between 'swimming_progression' and 'record_longevity', effectively increasing expected record longevity. The minimal improvement over the last decade supports the idea that the current record is near the upper bound of human performance, reducing the likelihood it will be surpassed. This strengthens the path from 'record_longevity' to 'outcome'. While 'elite_competition_frequency' still allows opportunities for attempts, the diminished gains from technique, training, and technology suggest fewer successful challenges to the record. Thus, the structural importance of 'swimming_progression' as a driver of change is reduced, increasing the probability that Hosszú’s record persists. The update is substantial but not maximal due to residual uncertainty about isolated breakthroughs or rule changes.

probe_results.probe_text: What if world records in elite swimming are not primarily determined by historical longevity trends, but instead are broken in sudden bursts due to unforeseen technological advances, such as new swimwear materials or pool design, making past record durations a poor predictor of future survival? In that case, even a historically long-standing record might be highly vulnerable regardless of average longevity.
probe_results.reasoning: The challenge undermines the reliability of 'record_longevity' as a predictor of the record's survival, which in turn weakens the causal path from 'record_longevity' to 'outcome'. If world records are instead broken in sudden bursts due to unpredictable technological advances, then historical trends in record longevity become less relevant. This strengthens the influence of exogenous shocks—such as new swimwear or pool designs—that are not well captured by the current model. Since 'record_longevity' was a key supporting factor in the original 0.32 estimate (implying some stability based on past patterns), its diminished explanatory power increases uncertainty and lowers confidence that the record will persist. The weakened path from 'record_longevity' to 'outcome' shifts the balance toward faster progression and external disruptions, indirectly amplifying the impact of 'swimming_progression' and reducing the likelihood of record survival. This justifies a downward adjustment to 0.22, reflecting greater vulnerability of the record to discontinuous breakthroughs.

probe_results.probe_text: Recent changes in the international swimming calendar have reduced the number of major competitions from six to three per year due to consolidation of events under the World Aquatics Championships and Olympic cycle. With fewer high-level meets where record attempts are attempted under peak conditions, the rate of swimming progression has slowed, suggesting that elite competition frequency may not be driving performance improvement as strongly as assumed.
probe_results.reasoning: The challenge to 'elite_competition_frequency' suggests that the number of major competitions has decreased, reducing opportunities for record attempts. This weakens the causal link from elite_competition_frequency to swimming_progression, implying that performance improvement may be slower than previously assumed. As a result, swimming_progression is reduced, which in turn increases record_longevity. With longer expected record durations due to fewer peak-performance opportunities, the likelihood that Katinka Hosszú's record persists until 2025-12-28 increases. This affects the path: elite_competition_frequency → swimming_progression → record_longevity → outcome. However, since Hosszú is likely retired (peripheral uncertainty in athlete_retirement_status) and cannot re-break her record, the main effect is passive preservation. The update is modest because elite_competition_frequency was not the dominant driver in the original model, and rule_stability and underlying progression trends still play significant roles.

probe_results.probe_text: Katinka Hosszú has officially retired from competitive swimming, but recent training logs and interviews suggest she may return to elite competition for a final season in 2025. If she comes out of retirement, her active status could allow her to defend or even re-break her own record, undermining the assumption that retirement permanently reduces her chances of retaining the record.
probe_results.reasoning: The challenge to 'athlete_retirement_status' affects a moderately important causal path. While Katinka Hosszú has officially retired, the possibility of a 2025 comeback reintroduces uncertainty into the 'athlete_retirement_status -> outcome' link. If she returns, she could actively defend or re-break her record, increasing its resilience. This weakens the assumption that retirement permanently reduces her chances of holding the record. However, the overall impact is limited because the likelihood of a successful comeback and record-breaking performance at that level remains uncertain, and other factors like swimming_progression and record_longevity still dominate the causal structure. Thus, the update is small but positive, reflecting a slightly higher chance of retention due to potential active defense of the record.

probe_results.probe_text: A 2024 study published in the Journal of Sports Sciences analyzed the last 20 years of elite swimming performances and found that advancements in underwater dolphin kick techniques, combined with optimized training regimens using AI-driven analytics, have accelerated the rate of improvement in the 200m individual medley by 1.8% per year since 2020—double the rate observed in the previous decade. This suggests that swimming progression is occurring faster than historically assumed, increasing the likelihood of record-breaking performances before 2025-12-28.
probe_results.reasoning: The new evidence directly challenges the 'swimming_progression' node by showing that performance improvement in the 200m individual medley has accelerated significantly since 2020. This strengthens the 'swimming_progression' → 'record_longevity' link, implying shorter expected record durations. Faster progression increases the likelihood of the current world record being broken before 2025-12-28, especially given the high frequency of elite competitions where such advancements can be deployed. This cascades through the causal network: reduced record longevity directly lowers the probability of Hosszú still holding the record, even if she remains the current holder and is active. Since swimming progression is a core driver of record longevity, and the observed acceleration is substantial (double the prior rate), the update is significant. The outcome probability is therefore substantially reduced from 0.32 to 0.18.

probe_results.probe_text: A 2024 longitudinal study published in the Journal of Sports Performance Analysis found that world records in long-course swimming have lasted an average of 7.3 years since 2000, with records set after 2010 showing increased durability due to maturation of technique and stricter oversight of performance-enhancing technologies. The study concludes that records established in the last decade are likely to persist longer than previously expected, reinforcing the structural stability of existing benchmarks like Hosszú's.
probe_results.reasoning: The new evidence directly strengthens the 'record_longevity' node, which is a key causal driver of the outcome. The study indicates that long-course swimming records set in the last decade, including Hosszú's (set in 2015), are more durable than historical averages suggest—averaging 7.3 years but with increased longevity post-2010 due to stabilized technique and regulatory oversight. This updates our understanding of 'record_longevity' upward, which in turn increases the expected survival probability of the current record until 2025-12-28. The causal path from 'swimming_progression' → 'record_longevity' is attenuated, as slower progression (due to maturation and rule stability) extends record lifespan. Since 'record_longevity' is a direct antecedent of 'outcome', and this new data provides empirical support for longer persistence of recent records, the probability is increased from 0.32 to 0.48. The shift is moderate because other factors—such as Hosszú's retirement status and elite competition frequency—still pose downward pressure on the likelihood of retention.

probe_results.probe_text: Recent data from the past decade shows that despite steady improvements in training and technology, the rate of world record breaks in the 200m individual medley has slowed, with no new records set since 2015. This suggests that swimming progression may have diminishing returns at the elite level, where physiological limits constrain further gains, weakening the causal link between performance improvement and record longevity.
probe_results.reasoning: The challenge weakens the causal link between swimming_progression and record_longevity, suggesting that performance improvements are not translating into frequent record breaks in this event, likely due to diminishing physiological returns. This implies that record_longevity may be greater than previously expected, even if swimming_progression continues. As a result, the current record held by Katinka Hosszú has a higher chance of persisting until 2025-12-28. This affects the path: swimming_progression -> record_longevity -> outcome, effectively strengthening the link between current_record_holder and outcome by increasing expected record longevity. The probability is updated upward, though not drastically, because other factors like elite_competition_frequency and potential rule changes still allow for a non-trivial chance the record will be broken.

probe_results.probe_text: Recent data from World Aquatics shows that 80% of long course swimming records set between 2015 and 2020 were broken within five years, despite historically long record longevity in the 2000s. This accelerating trend suggests that past record longevity is no longer predictive of future survival, especially for records set before the latest wave of training and technology advances. As a result, relying on historical record longevity may overestimate the durability of Hosszú's 2015 record.
probe_results.reasoning: The challenge directly undermines the causal link 'record_longevity -> outcome' by showing that recent trends in swimming performance have drastically shortened the lifespan of world records, even though historical longevity might suggest otherwise. Hosszú's record was set in 2015, and the evidence indicates that 80% of records from that period were broken within five years. This weakens the expectation that her record will survive until 2025-12-28, despite prior assumptions about record durability. The path from record_longevity to outcome is critical in the network, and its erosion significantly reduces the estimated probability. Additionally, this trend implies that swimming_progression is accelerating, further diminishing record longevity. Since Hosszú is likely retired (athlete_retirement_status), she cannot re-break her own record, increasing the chances it will be overtaken. The combined effect of faster progression and reduced record survival justifies a substantial downward update.

probe_results.probe_text: Even if Katinka Hosszú retires, she could still indirectly influence the likelihood of her record being broken by inspiring targeted training programs or pacing strategies in younger swimmers aiming to surpass her time, meaning retirement does not necessarily increase the vulnerability of the record. In some cases, retired record-holders' times become psychological benchmarks that actually slow down subsequent record progression due to their symbolic status.
probe_results.reasoning: The challenge to the causal link 'athlete_retirement_status -> outcome' suggests that retirement does not necessarily make a record more vulnerable — and could even reinforce its durability through psychological or motivational effects on future swimmers. This weakens the original assumption that retirement inherently increases the likelihood of the record being broken. The revised understanding introduces a countervailing mechanism: a retired record-holder's time may become a symbolic benchmark that paradoxically slows progression by setting a clear target that is difficult to surpass, or inspires training adaptations that take time to mature. While this does not eliminate the general trend that active record-holders can defend their records, it reduces the strength of the 'athlete_retirement_status' node as a driver of record turnover. As a result, the outcome (Hosszú still holding the record) becomes slightly more likely, even if she retires. This affects the downstream path from athlete_retirement_status to outcome, partially decoupling retirement from record vulnerability. However, since this node was only one of several inputs into the outcome — and other factors like swimming_progression and record_longevity remain dominant — the update is modest. Hence, the probability increases slightly from 0.32 to 0.34.

probe_results.probe_text: Suppose the world record in the 200m individual medley has remained unbroken for an unusually long time. This stagnation may trigger intensified focus from coaches, athletes, and sports scientists on cracking the record, leading to targeted innovations in training and technique. Could this mean that long-standing records actually accelerate swimming progression, rather than being merely eroded by it?
probe_results.reasoning: The challenge suggests that long-standing records may accelerate swimming progression due to increased focus and innovation aimed at breaking them. This reverses the original causal direction of 'swimming_progression -> record_longevity' by introducing a feedback loop where record longevity actively promotes faster progression. As a result, the durability of Hosszú's record (record_longevity) becomes a risk factor rather than a protective one—the longer it stands, the more likely targeted efforts will emerge to break it. This weakens the support for the outcome through the record_longevity node and strengthens the pressure from swimming_progression. Since the record has already persisted for several years, it may now be entering a phase of heightened vulnerability. This shifts the probability downward, though not drastically, because the structural advantages of rule stability and Hosszú's retirement (reducing re-breaking potential) still provide some insulation. The causal path from record_longevity to outcome is thus partially inverted, increasing the likelihood the record will fall by 2025-12-28.

probe_results.probe_text: Imagine that, historically, world records in elite swimming were broken much more frequently—say, every 1.5 years on average instead of every 3—due to rapid advancements in training and swimwear. Would it still be as likely that Katinka Hosszú set and retained the 200m IM record in the first place, given that such a volatile environment would make any single record less likely to emerge and stabilize? This suggests that the typical longevity of records causally influences which athletes end up being record holders, as only in relatively stable periods do records persist long enough to be set and recognized.
probe_results.reasoning: The challenge suggests that record_longevity causally influences current_record_holder, rather than being a downstream consequence of it — implying a feedback loop where the stability of the environment (and thus expected record longevity) affects the likelihood that any record, including Hosszú's, is set and persists long enough to be recognized. This reverses part of the original causal direction and introduces a selection effect: in environments with short record longevity (e.g., records broken every 1.5 years), it is less likely that a record would have been set and held by any one athlete, including Hosszú, in the first place. However, since we observe that Hosszú *does* currently hold the record, this suggests the environment was relatively stable when she set it. But if the baseline swimming progression is accelerating (due to training, technology, etc.), then even if she set the record during a brief stable window, the future longevity of that record is still low. Therefore, the revised causal understanding strengthens the influence of swimming_progression on both record_longevity and the credibility of current_record_holder, making it less likely the record will survive until 2025-12-28. This weakens the predictive power of 'current_record_holder' as a stable node and amplifies the downstream risk from fast swimming_progression. As a result, the probability that Hosszú still holds the record in 2025 decreases.

probe_results.probe_text: Suppose new data shows that world records in elite swimming are being broken 40% faster than in the past decade due to advances in training and underwater technology. How does this accelerated progression affect the likelihood that Katinka Hosszú remains an active competitor? Consider that shorter record longevity may demotivate elite swimmers from continuing if they perceive their achievements as more easily surpassed.
probe_results.reasoning: The new information indicates that swimming records are being broken 40% faster due to advances in training and technology, which directly strengthens the 'swimming_progression' node and weakens 'record_longevity'. This accelerates the expected obsolescence of existing records, including Hosszú's. Crucially, the challenge introduces a novel causal path: shortened record longevity may demotivate elite swimmers like Hosszú, reducing the likelihood she remains active (affecting 'athlete_retirement_status'). If she retires, she cannot re-break her own record, increasing the chance it will be overtaken. Thus, the indirect link from 'record_longevity' to 'athlete_retirement_status'—previously unmodeled—is now activated, creating a feedback loop where rapid progression not only shortens record lifespan but also potentially removes the record-holder from competition. This双重 effect significantly weakens the 'outcome' node. Therefore, the probability that Hosszú still holds the record in 2025 is reduced from 0.32 to 0.24.

probe_results.probe_text: Consider the possibility of a technological advancement in swimwear material that significantly enhances buoyancy and reduces drag, approved by FINA before 2025. Such a change could accelerate performance improvements across elite swimming, increasing the likelihood that existing records, including Hosszú's, are broken even if other factors like rule stability and competition frequency remain unchanged. This represents a shock to the system not captured by the current network.
probe_results.reasoning: The introduction of a technological advancement in swimwear that enhances performance represents a direct shock to the swimming_progression node, accelerating the rate at which performances improve. This bypasses the existing moderating effect of rule_stability, which previously helped constrain artificial performance gains. Although rule_stability remains intact in form, the interpretation and enforcement of what constitutes legal equipment may shift, effectively weakening its causal influence. The new technology increases the likelihood of record-breaking performances independently of athlete effort or training advances, thereby reducing record_longevity and directly decreasing the probability that Hosszú's record remains unbroken. This shock strengthens the pathway from elite_competition_frequency to swimming_progression, as more competitions amplify the diffusion and optimization of the new swimwear. Since the outcome depends heavily on record_longevity and Hosszú's inability to re-break her record if retired (a likely scenario by 2025), the unanticipated acceleration in swimming_progression significantly undermines the durability of the current record. This affects a core causal path in the model and justifies a substantial downward revision.

probe_results.probe_text: Suppose a new technological advancement, such as a significantly more efficient swimsuit design, is approved by FINA in 2024. This leads to a wave of record-breaking performances across multiple swimming disciplines, including the 200m individual medley. How does this affect the likelihood that Katinka Hosszú's record will still stand by the end of 2025?
probe_results.reasoning: The introduction of a significantly more efficient swimsuit design approved by FINA in 2024 directly impacts the 'rule_stability' node, weakening it. Reduced rule stability enables artificial performance enhancements, which strengthens the 'swimming_progression' node. Faster swimming progression shortens 'record_longevity', reducing the expected lifespan of existing records. This effect is amplified if 'elite_competition_frequency' remains high, providing many opportunities for the new technology to be exploited. Since Katinka Hosszú is likely retired or near retirement (affecting 'athlete_retirement_status'), she cannot re-break her record to match the new performance baseline. Thus, the causal path from rule_stability → swimming_progression → record_longevity → outcome is critically weakened, substantially decreasing the likelihood her record survives. The structural challenge targets a core mechanism in the model, warranting a significant downward update.

probe_results.probe_text: In 2024, a new training facility dedicated to adolescent swimmers was opened in Budapest, named in honor of Katinka Hosszú. The center focuses on developing young talent in individual medley events and has received significant government funding.
probe_results.reasoning: The opening of a training facility in honor of Katinka Hosszú in Budapest, while symbolically significant, does not directly affect any of the causal paths in the network leading to whether she still holds the world record in 2025. The facility may influence long-term talent development (potentially affecting swimming_progression or elite_competition_frequency in the distant future), but it does not alter the current record longevity, her retirement status, the immediate rate of performance improvement, or rule stability. Since the outcome is assessed in December 2025—just over two years from now—the impact of a new training center on adolescent swimmers is too indirect and delayed to meaningfully shift the probability. No critical causal link is altered, and the peripheral nature of this development warrants no update to the estimate.

probe_results.probe_text: In 2024, a new underwater camera system was introduced at major swimming events to enhance broadcast quality and provide real-time stroke analysis for commentators. While widely adopted for television coverage, the technology does not influence swimmer performance or timing accuracy.
probe_results.reasoning: The introduction of a new underwater camera system for broadcast and commentary purposes does not affect any of the causal paths in the model. It does not influence swimmer performance (swimming_progression), timing accuracy, rule_stability, or competition frequency. It also does not impact Katinka Hosszú's retirement status or the typical longevity of records. Since the technology is purely observational and does not feed into training improvements or performance enhancements at scale, it leaves all key drivers of record survival unchanged. Therefore, the probability remains unaffected.

probe_results.probe_text: The International Swimming Federation (FINA) has announced the addition of two new World Aquatics Championships within the 2023–2025 cycle, increasing the number of elite-level long course competitions where swimmers can challenge records. This change reflects a broader strategy to expand the global competitive calendar, providing more frequent opportunities for record attempts in events like the 200m individual medley.
